section: Carbon and its Compounds: Bonding and Versatility

concepts.lesson: Carbon has 4 electrons in its outermost shell. To achieve stability, it shares its valence electrons with other atoms instead of losing or gaining them (which would require too much energy). This sharing of electrons results in covalent bonds. Covalent compounds generally have low melting/boiling points and are poor conductors of electricity.
concepts.real_example: The formation of a Methane ($CH_4$) molecule where one carbon atom shares electrons with four hydrogen atoms.
key_points: Tetravalency: Carbon can bond with four other atoms
Covalent bonds involve electron sharing
Poor conductors because no charged ions are formed

concepts.lesson: Carbon's uniqueness comes from Catenation—the ability to form long chains or rings by bonding with other carbon atoms. Additionally, carbon compounds can exhibit Structural Isomerism, where compounds have the same molecular formula but different structures/arrangements.
concepts.real_example: Butane and Iso-butane both have the formula $C_4H_{10}$ but different carbon skeletons.
key_points: Catenation: Self-linking property of carbon
Isomers have different physical and chemical properties
Carbon can form single, double, or triple bonds

concepts.lesson: Hydrocarbons are compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen. Saturated hydrocarbons (Alkanes) have only single bonds and are relatively unreactive. Unsaturated hydrocarbons (Alkenes and Alkynes) contain double or triple bonds and are more reactive.
concepts.real_example: Ethane ($C_2H_6$) is saturated, while Ethene ($C_2H_4$) is unsaturated.
key_points: Alkanes formula: $C_nH_{2n+2}$
Alkenes formula: $C_nH_{2n}$
Alkynes formula: $C_nH_{2n-2}$

concepts.lesson: A series of compounds in which the same functional group substitutes for hydrogen in a carbon chain. Members of a series have similar chemical properties but show a gradual change in physical properties (like boiling point) as molecular mass increases.
concepts.real_example: The alcohol series: $CH_3OH, C_2H_5OH, C_3H_7OH$, etc., where each member differs by a $-CH_2$ unit.
key_points: Difference between successive members is 14u in mass
Same functional group implies similar chemical reactivity
Melting/boiling points increase with chain length

concepts.lesson: An atom or group of atoms that replaces hydrogen in a hydrocarbon and determines the chemical properties of the compound. Common groups include Halogens, Alcohols ($-OH$), Aldehydes ($-CHO$), Ketones ($>C=O$), and Carboxylic acids ($-COOH$).
concepts.real_example: The presence of the $-OH$ group makes a compound an alcohol (e.g., Ethanol).
key_points: Heteroatoms like $O, N, S$, and Halogens provide specific properties
The functional group is the 'reactive' part of the molecule
Nomenclature (naming) is based on these groups

concepts.lesson: Carbon compounds burn in oxygen to release $CO_2$, water, and heat (Combustion). Unsaturated hydrocarbons undergo Addition reactions (like Hydrogenation) to become saturated, while saturated ones undergo Substitution reactions in the presence of sunlight.
concepts.real_example: Hydrogenation of vegetable oils (unsaturated) into vanaspati ghee (saturated) using a nickel catalyst.
key_points: Saturated fats burn with a clean blue flame
Unsaturated fats burn with a yellow sooty flame
Addition reactions are used to test for unsaturation

concepts.lesson: Ethanol is a liquid at room temperature and is a good solvent. Ethanoic acid (Acetic acid) is a weak acid; its 5-8% solution in water is called vinegar. They react together to form Esters, which have sweet fruity smells.
concepts.real_example: Esterification reaction used in making perfumes and flavoring agents.
key_points: Absolute alcohol is 100% pure ethanol
Glacial acetic acid is pure ethanoic acid that freezes in winter
Saponification is the reverse of esterification, used to make soap

concepts.lesson: Soaps are sodium/potassium salts of long-chain fatty acids. They have a hydrophilic (water-loving) head and a hydrophobic (water-hating) tail. In water, they form clusters called 'micelles' to trap oily dirt. Detergents work in hard water, unlike soaps which form 'scum'.
concepts.real_example: Using soap to wash away a grease stain by forming an emulsion.
key_points: Micelles point tails inward toward the oil
Hard water contains $Ca$ and $Mg$ ions which react with soap
Detergents are ammonium or sulphonate salts
